spec_match.rationale: Probes 21 at-rules across 6 categories with CSS.supports('at-rule(@x)') as defined by css-conditional-5, including the descriptor-form via the `literal` field (`@container style(--theme: dark)`) and intentionally-removed legacy rules (@viewport, @document).

interactive.rationale: Category filter buttons, sentinel @media check, totals, and a user-input custom-probe field that injects new cells into the grid. Real CSS.supports() invocations throughout.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Index promises 'live grid of every interesting at-rule probed with CSS.supports("at-rule(@x)") in your browser, filterable by category, with a custom probe input for testing unusual at-rules including style queries' — page delivers.

edge_cases.rationale: Sentinel @media check detects browsers without `at-rule()` support entirely (good). Custom probe accepts arbitrary input but doesn't sanitize — pasting `@x); body{display:none}` would inject into the displayed string. The legacy @viewport / @document rows correctly come back red, which teaches the still-rejected boundary.

design_system.rationale: All colours from CSS variables; yes/no cells use --accent-emerald / --accent-rose with --text-black; AA-clean. No raw hex.

openQuestions.title: Escape user input on custom probes
openQuestions.detail: The custom-probe input is fed verbatim into `cell.innerHTML` via the verdict line and into the `desc` field. It's not actively dangerous (the harmful path would need to inject HTML, not CSS), but `` in the input would currently render. Wrap the user-provided value through an escaper before stringifying into the innerHTML.

openQuestions.title: Add the descriptor-form probe to the picker
openQuestions.detail: `@container style(...)` is the only descriptor-form probe in the preset list — but the user-input row only takes a bare name. A second input or a 'descriptor' field would let users probe `at-rule(@font-face; font-display: swap)` and other descriptor checks the spec supports.

openQuestions.title: Show category counts on the filter buttons
openQuestions.detail: Filter buttons say 'layout', 'container', 'conditional', etc. but no count. Inline `(9)` next to each filter would help visitors gauge the matrix breadth before clicking and would surface the 'legacy' category as small-but-real.

openQuestions.title: Add `@function`, `@if`, `@else` for upcoming CSS
openQuestions.detail: css-functions-1 introduces `@function` (and the conditional working group is exploring `@if`/`@else`). Adding red cells for these now would let the page act as a 'what's coming next' radar — and would update naturally as browsers ship them.

summary: Most comprehensive of the five concepts for this feature — 21 probes across 6 categories, descriptor form included, custom input, legacy rows for boundary-teaching. Main fix: escape user input on the custom-probe path so a paste with HTML can't break out of the cell.
